skill.name: harness-model-protocol
skill.description: Analyze how agent frameworks translate messages, tool calls, streaming events, and provider-specific LLM protocols.
skill.summary: Maps the protocol layer between an agent harness and LLM providers.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, weak-crypto, sensitive-data, and prompt-extraction patterns, but review found them in markdown prose, tables, placeholders, and illustrative Python snippets. No executable script, credential access, malicious prompt injection, or exfiltration behavior was found in the skill. The remaining low risk is expected filesystem output because the skill instructs the agent to save a markdown report.

low_findings.title: Expected Filesystem Output
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to save a generated markdown analysis under forensics-output/frameworks/{framework}/phase2. This is normal for a documentation workflow, but users should confirm the target path before writing in sensitive repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write instruction is explicit and scoped to a report path. It does not request deletion, broad overwrite, secret collection, or hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous external command alerts correspond to fenced code blocks, inline code formatting, and file glob examples in SKILL.md. They are documentation examples, not runnable Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside markdown fences or inline code spans. No executable skill script or shell command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network and API Examples Are Illustrative
low_findings.description: The network alerts are from example provider request methods and client calls used to explain LLM protocol translation. The skill contains no endpoint, webhook, credential transmission, or executable network client.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Python examples embedded in documentation. They describe normal provider adapter behavior and do not execute from the skill package.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prompt Security Terms Are Analytical Examples
low_findings.description: The prompt-related alerts appear in a section explaining system prompt assembly and behavioral constraints. The text does not instruct the evaluator or runtime agent to ignore higher-priority instructions or reveal hidden prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The phrases are framed as topics to analyze in target frameworks. They are not written as active override instructions aimed at this audit or the hosting model.

content.value_statement: Agent frameworks hide important differences between model APIs, tool calls, and streaming formats. This skill guides a structured review of those protocol layers and produces a focused architecture report.
actual_capabilities: Identifies wire format families such as OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or custom protocols.
Compares how frameworks encode tool schemas, tool calls, and tool results.
Reviews streaming event handling, including partial tool call accumulation.
Documents system prompt assembly, scratchpads, interrupts, and conversation state patterns.
Maps provider abstraction strategies and feature support across LLM backends.
Produces a structured markdown report for protocol and architecture decisions.
limitations: Requires access to the target framework source code or clear code excerpts.
Does not execute tests or verify live provider API behavior by itself.
May need human judgment when framework adapters are generated or highly dynamic.
Focuses on harness-to-model protocols, not tool implementation internals.

use_cases.title: Audit an agent framework integration
use_cases.description: Trace how messages, tools, and streaming responses move between framework objects and provider APIs.

use_cases.title: Compare provider adapter designs
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether a framework uses thin adapters, unified clients, or gateway translation for multiple model providers.

use_cases.title: Prepare implementation guidance
use_cases.description: Extract positive patterns and anti-patterns for a new agent framework or model gateway.

prompt_templates.title: Map the message protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Use harness-model-protocol to analyze this framework. Start by identifying the internal message type, supported providers, and native wire formats.

prompt_templates.title: Review tool call encoding
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze how this framework sends tool schemas to the model and parses tool call responses. Include tool result attribution and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate streaming behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use harness-model-protocol to examine streaming events. Focus on token deltas, tool call fragments, accumulator logic, and finalization behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Build a provider abstraction assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare this framework against OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and local model support. Explain abstraction strategy, graceful degradation, and protocol trade-offs.

output_examples.input: Analyze a framework that supports OpenAI and Anthropic chat models.
output: A protocol classification that names the wire format families and adapter strategy.
A tool-calling section that explains schema transmission and response parsing.
A streaming section that documents event types and partial tool call handling.

output_examples.input: Review provider abstraction for a new model gateway.
output: A provider feature matrix covering streaming, tool choice, parallel tools, and structured output.
Positive patterns to adopt and protocol limitations to address.
Code reference notes pointing to the relevant framework files and lines.

best_practices: Start with message type definitions before reviewing provider-specific adapters.
Verify tool call IDs and tool result roles across multi-turn conversations.
Separate documented protocol facts from design recommendations in the final report.
anti_patterns: Do not treat provider-native formats as identical across OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini.
Do not ignore streaming tool argument fragments when evaluating tool reliability.
Do not document architecture claims without file and line references from the target framework.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes the protocol layer between an agent framework and LLM providers, including messages, tools, streaming, and adapters.

faq.question: Does it inspect tool implementation code?
faq.answer: Only when tool implementation affects wire protocol behavior. It mainly focuses on harness-to-model translation.

faq.question: Which providers does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes patterns for OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and local providers when the target framework supports them.

faq.question: What output does it produce?
faq.answer: It produces a structured markdown analysis with protocol classification, code references, feature tables, and design implications.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples and one expected report-writing instruction, with no malicious behavior.
